questions.question: What is the main modifiable risk factor in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Elevated intraocular pressure is the main modifiable risk factor, since its reduction delays the progression of glaucomatous damage.

questions.question: What structure of the eye is directly damaged by elevated intraocular pressure in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Glaucomatous damage directly affects the optic nerve, specifically the retinal ganglion cells and their axons.

questions.question: What is the first-line medical treatment for open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues (e.g. latanoprost) are the first-line treatment due to their effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure with a single daily dose.

questions.question: What finding is characteristic in the optic disc of open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Increased papillary excavation (cup/disc ratio > 0.5 or asymmetric) is the classic fundoscopic finding due to axon loss.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism by which elevated intraocular pressure damages the optic nerve?
questions.explanation: Elevated intraocular pressure mechanically compresses the axons of ganglion cells in the lamina cribrosa, altering axoplasmic flow and causing apoptosis.

questions.question: What imaging test is used to evaluate the progression of glaucomatous damage?
questions.explanation: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) measures the thickness of the retinal nerve fiber layer and ganglion cell complex, quantifying structural damage.

questions.question: What gonioscopic finding defines open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma is characterized by an open iridocorneal angle on gonioscopy, which differentiates this entity from closed-angle glaucoma.

questions.question: What is the structure directly evaluated with gonioscopy?
questions.explanation: Gonioscopy allows you to directly visualize the iridocorneal angle, formed by the root of the iris and the peripheral cornea, key to classifying glaucoma.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is not part of the iridocorneal angle?
questions.explanation: The iridocorneal angle includes the trabecular meshwork, Schlemm's canal, and the ciliary body, but the lens is an internal structure of the eye, not the angle.

questions.question: What is the first line of pharmacological treatment for open angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues, such as latanoprost, are considered first-line due to their effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure and their safety profile.

questions.question: What main pathophysiological mechanism causes damage in open-angle glaucoma?
options: Chronic inflammation of the uveal tract
questions.explanation: Increased intraocular pressure is the main risk factor and pathophysiological mechanism, although resistance to the flow of aqueous humor in the trabecular meshwork is the primary event.

questions.question: What is the characteristic clinical sign of open-angle glaucoma in early stages?
questions.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma is usually asymptomatic in early stages, which delays diagnosis and makes screening essential in at-risk patients.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of open angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma is characterized by a chronic increase in intraocular pressure due to resistance to drainage of aqueous humor through the trabecular meshwork, leading to optic nerve damage and peripheral visual field loss.

questions.question: Which test is the gold standard for diagnosing gradual peripheral visual field loss?
questions.explanation: Automated perimetry is the reference test to document and quantify peripheral visual field loss in open-angle glaucoma, allowing the detection of arcuate and nasal step defects.

questions.question: What is the first-line medical treatment for open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues (such as latanoprost) are the first-line treatment due to their effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure with a single daily dose and good profile of systemic adverse effects.

questions.question: Which campimetric finding is characteristic of early open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: The arcuate scotoma (or arc-shaped defect) is a classic finding in the perimetry of open-angle glaucoma, reflecting damage to the nerve fibers that follow the temporal arc of the optic nerve.

questions.question: What is the most important risk factor for developing open angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Ocular hypertension (elevated intraocular pressure) is the main and modifiable risk factor for open-angle glaucoma, although not all patients with ocular hypertension develop the disease.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is primarily affected in the visual loss of open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Open-angle glaucoma progressively damages the optic nerve, specifically the retinal nerve fibers that originate in the periphery, which explains the gradual loss of the peripheral visual field.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic ophthalmoscopic sign of open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Progressive papillary cupping is the hallmark funduscopic finding of open-angle glaucoma, reflecting chronic loss of nerve fibers and axonal damage to the optic nerve.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains papillary excavation in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Apoptosis of retinal ganglion cells leads to the loss of axons in the optic nerve, generating a progressive papillary excavation observable in the fundus.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for developing progressive papillary excavation?
questions.explanation: Elevated intraocular pressure is the most important risk factor for the progression of papillary cupping in open-angle glaucoma, although it is not the only determinant.

questions.question: What study confirms the progression of papillary excavation?
questions.explanation: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) allows quantification of the thickness of the nerve fiber layer and neuroretinal ring, objectively documenting the progression of papillary cupping.

questions.question: Which fundus finding is typically associated with glaucomatous papillary cupping?
questions.explanation: Nasal displacement of the retinal vessels is a classic sign of advanced papillary excavation, due to the loss of neural tissue in the optic disc that alters the vascular architecture.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment to stop the progression of papillary cupping?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues are the first-line topical treatment to reduce intraocular pressure and slow the progression of papillary cupping in open-angle glaucoma.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for open angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is the most important risk factor for the development of open-angle glaucoma, with its prevalence increasing significantly after age 60.

questions.question: From what age does the risk of open-angle glaucoma increase significantly?
questions.explanation: The risk of open-angle glaucoma increases markedly after age 60, with advanced age being the main risk factor for this disease.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism is related to advanced age in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is associated with progressive loss of retinal ganglion cells, which contributes to the glaucomatous damage characteristic of open-angle glaucoma.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a modifiable risk factor for open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Advanced age is a non-modifiable risk factor for open-angle glaucoma, unlike intraocular pressure, use of corticosteroids or obesity, which can be intervened on.

questions.question: Which ophthalmological examination is most frequently recommended in elderly patients to detect glaucoma?
options: Fundus of eye with intraocular pressure
questions.explanation: In elderly patients, fundus examination combined with intraocular pressure measurement is the most recommended screening test to detect open-angle glaucoma early.

questions.question: What is the first line of pharmacological treatment for open-angle glaucoma in the elderly?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues, such as latanoprost, are the first line of treatment for open-angle glaucoma, even in older adults, due to their effectiveness and safety profile.

questions.question: What risk factor is associated with a higher prevalence of open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: High myopia, especially greater than 6 diopters, is an established risk factor for primary open-angle glaucoma, probably due to biomechanical alterations of the optic nerve.

questions.question: Which of the following ethnic groups is at greatest risk for open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Afro-descendant race is an independent risk factor for primary open-angle glaucoma, with higher prevalence, later diagnosis, and worse visual prognosis.

questions.question: What structural alteration of the eye is related to myopia and open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Posterior scleral thinning in high myopia generates biomechanical changes that increase the susceptibility of the optic nerve to glaucomatous damage, even at average intraocular pressures.

questions.question: In Afro-descendant patients with open-angle glaucoma, which finding is most common?
questions.explanation: Afro-descendant patients usually present with open-angle glaucoma in more advanced stages at the time of diagnosis, which worsens the prognosis and limits therapeutic options.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism links high myopia with open-angle glaucoma?
options: Mechanical stress on the lamina cribrosa
questions.explanation: In high myopia, axial lengthening and scleral thinning generate greater mechanical stress on the lamina cribrosa, facilitating glaucomatous axonal damage even with normal intraocular pressures.

questions.question: Which of the following treatments for open-angle glaucoma may be least effective in African descendants?
questions.explanation: Topical beta-blockers show less hypotensive efficacy in patients of African descent, possibly due to pharmacogenetic differences in the adrenergic receptors of the eye.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of prostaglandin analogues in glaucoma?
options: Decrease the production of aqueous humor
Increase the production of aqueous humor
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues, such as latanoprost, act by increasing uveoscleral flow, which reduces intraocular pressure by facilitating the outflow of aqueous humor.

questions.question: Which prostaglandin analogue is considered first-line in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost is the prostaglandin analogue most used as first-line treatment due to its effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure and its favorable safety profile.

questions.question: What is the most common adverse effect of prostaglandin analogues?
questions.explanation: Iris hyperpigmentation is a common adverse effect of prostaglandin analogues, due to stimulation of melanogenesis in iris melanocytes.

questions.question: What relative contraindication do prostaglandin analogues have?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues are relatively contraindicated in active ocular inflammation, such as uveitis, because they can exacerbate the inflammatory process.

questions.question: What route of administration is used for prostaglandin analogues in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues are administered topically ophthalmically in the form of drops, which allows direct local action on the eye with minimal systemic absorption.

questions.question: What is the main objective of treatment with prostaglandin analogues in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: The primary goal of prostaglandin analogues is to reduce intraocular pressure, which delays the progression of glaucomatous damage and preserves visual function.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of latanoprost in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analogue that increases uveoscleral flow, reducing intraocular pressure by facilitating the outflow of aqueous humor through this alternative route.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic adverse effect of latanoprost?
questions.explanation: Irreversible hyperpigmentation of the iris is the most distinctive adverse effect of latanoprost, due to the stimulation of melanocytes by prostaglandin, although other effects such as macular edema are less common.

questions.question: How often is latanoprost typically administered in open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost is administered once a day, usually at night, due to its long duration of action. More frequent doses do not improve effectiveness and increase the risk of adverse effects.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs belongs to the same therapeutic class as latanoprost?
questions.explanation: Bimatoprost is another prostaglandin analog, just like latanoprost, while timolol is a beta-blocker, brinzolamide a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, and pilocarpine a cholinergic.

questions.question: What absolute contraindication is associated with the use of latanoprost?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost is contraindicated in pregnancy due to its effect on uterine smooth muscle, which can induce contractions. Asthma is a contraindication for beta blockers, not prostaglandins.

questions.question: What is the main indication for latanoprost in ophthalmology?
questions.explanation: Latanoprost is indicated as a first-line treatment to reduce intraocular pressure in open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension, not in infections, cataracts or detachments.

questions.question: What type of drug increases uveoscleral drainage?
questions.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues, such as latanoprost, increase uveoscleral drainage of aqueous humor, reducing intraocular pressure.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of uveoscleral drainage?
options: Active secretion of the vitreous body
questions.explanation: Uveoscleral drainage occurs when aqueous humor flows through the spaces of the ciliary muscle toward the suprachoroid, bypassing Schlemm's canal.

questions.question: What percentage of aqueous drainage corresponds to the uveoscleral pathway in young people?
questions.explanation: In young individuals, the uveoscleral pathway represents approximately 25% of the total aqueous humor drainage, decreasing with age.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is directly involved in uveoscleral drainage?
questions.explanation: Uveoscleral drainage depends on the space between the fibers of the ciliary muscle and the suprachoroid, through which the aqueous humor is reabsorbed.

questions.question: What effect does age have on uveoscleral drainage?
questions.explanation: With age, uveoscleral drainage decreases due to structural changes in the ciliary muscle and suprachoroid, contributing to glaucoma.

questions.question: Which drug does NOT act by increasing uveoscleral drainage?
questions.explanation: Timolol is a beta blocker that reduces the production of aqueous humor, it does not increase uveoscleral drainage, unlike prostaglandin analogues.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of topical beta-blockers in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Topical beta blockers reduce aqueous humor production by blocking beta-adrenergic receptors in the ciliary body, lowering intraocular pressure.

questions.question: What type of receptors do topical beta-blockers block to reduce aqueous humor?
questions.explanation: Topical beta-blockers act primarily on beta-2 adrenergic receptors in the nonpigmented epithelium of the ciliary body, inhibiting the production of aqueous humor.

questions.question: What is a common systemic adverse effect of topical beta-blockers?
questions.explanation: Bradycardia is a common systemic adverse effect due to the absorption of the beta-blocker through the conjunctiva and its action on cardiac beta-1 receptors.

questions.question: Which topical beta blocker is most frequently used as first line in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Topical timolol is the beta-blocker most used as first line in open-angle glaucoma due to its effectiveness in reducing intraocular pressure and its safety profile.

questions.question: What ocular structure is responsible for producing aqueous humor?
questions.explanation: The ciliary body, specifically its non-pigmented epithelium, is responsible for the production of aqueous humor, a process inhibited by topical beta-blockers.

questions.question: What is the absolute contraindication to using topical beta blockers?
questions.explanation: Bronchial asthma is an absolute contraindication for topical beta-blockers because they can trigger severe bronchospasm by blocking pulmonary beta-2 receptors.

questions.question: What is the main objective of trabeculectomy in open angle glaucoma?
options: Reduce the production of aqueous humor
questions.explanation: Trabeculectomy creates a new drainage pathway for aqueous humor, reducing intraocular pressure by facilitating its exit from the anterior chamber to the subconjunctival space.

questions.question: When is trabeculectomy indicated in open angle glaucoma?
options: After failure of medical and laser treatment
As initial treatment in all patients
Only in patients under 40 years of age
questions.explanation: Trabeculectomy is reserved for cases where maximum tolerable medical treatment and laser trabeculoplasty fail to adequately control intraocular pressure.

questions.question: Which early complication is most characteristic of trabeculectomy?
questions.explanation: Severe ocular hypotension is a common early complication due to excessive filtration of aqueous humor through the scleral flap, which can cause hypotonic maculopathy.

questions.question: What antimetabolite agent is used during trabeculectomy to improve the success rate?
questions.explanation: Mitomycin C is applied intraoperatively to inhibit fibroblast proliferation and reduce scarring of the filtering bleb, improving long-term patency.

questions.question: Which of the following findings suggests failure of trabeculectomy?
options: Flat blister and elevated intraocular pressure
questions.explanation: A flat (non-elevated) bleb along with elevated intraocular pressure indicates obstruction of the flow of aqueous humor into the subconjunctival space, suggesting surgical failure.

questions.question: What structure is surgically created during trabeculectomy to drain aqueous humor?
questions.explanation: Trabeculectomy creates a subconjunctival filtering bleb, where aqueous humor accumulates under the conjunctiva and is reabsorbed by the episcleral vessels, reducing intraocular pressure.

questions.question: What parameter does Goldmann applanation tonometry directly measure?
questions.explanation: Goldmann applanation tonometry measures intraocular pressure in mmHg, being the reference standard for the diagnosis and monitoring of glaucoma.

questions.question: What is the main objective of computerized campimetry in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Computerized campimetry evaluates visual function by detecting and quantifying visual field defects, essential for monitoring glaucomatous progression.

questions.question: What structure does optical coherence tomography (OCT) evaluate in glaucoma?
questions.explanation: OCT measures the thickness of the retinal nerve fiber layer and the morphology of the optic nerve head, detecting early structural damage in glaucoma.

questions.question: Which campimetric finding is characteristic of early open-angle glaucoma?
questions.explanation: Nasal arcuate scotoma, which follows the pattern of nerve fibers, is a typical and early field defect of open-angle glaucoma.

questions.question: What is the usefulness of corneal pachymetry in glaucoma monitoring?
questions.explanation: Pachymetry measures central corneal thickness, which allows the intraocular pressure obtained by tonometry to be corrected, since thicker corneas overestimate IOP.

questions.question: Which OCT parameter best correlates with glaucoma progression?
questions.explanation: Progressive reduction in retinal nerve fiber layer thickness on OCT is a sensitive structural marker of glaucomatous progression.
